bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Grew up in a military family living in Southern California, New Mexico, New Jersey, Puerto Rico, Hawaii and San Francisco
  • Has an identical twin brother who competed with him at Vanderbilt
  • Former Division 1 soccer player that converted to beach handball after moving to Los Angeles
  • Was named San Francisco High School Player of the Year, helping his team win back to back championships, the first in 32 years
  • Has the nickname “Beast Mode,” which was started by his Team USA teammates during the filming of a sports competition show hosted by JJ Watt on FOX
  • Works as a UX Designer and helped create one of the most-played video games ever made at the time, which was added to the Guinness Book of World records (CityVille by Zynga).
Career Highlights

  • 2021 SoCal Beach Handball Championships - 2nd
  • 2021 EHF Champions Cup - 5th (Sicily, Italy)
  • 2021 Torneo Internacional De España Gran Canaria - 5th (Gran Canary Islands, Spain)
  • 2020 Copa Internacional de Balonmano Playa Volcán - 1st, Best Pivot
  • 2019 North American and the Caribbean (NAC) Beach Handball Championship - 1st (Trinidad & Tobago)
  • 2019 ANOC World Beach Games - 11th (Doha, Qatar)
  • 2019 SoCal Beach Handball Championships - 1st
  • 2019 PGNiG Summer Superliga Płock - 2nd, Best Left Wing (Poland)
  • 2019 Copa Internacional de Balonmano Playa Volcán - 3rd
  • 2018 SoCal Beach Handball Championships - 3rd
  • 2018 Pan American Beach Handball Championships - 3rd (Alternate)
  • 2018 Taça Kika Invitational (João Pessoa, Brazil)
  • 2017 SoCal Beach Handball Championships - 3rd

Quick Facts

  • Sport: Snowboarding

  • Specialty: Halfpipe

Biographical Information

Shaun White began snowboarding at a young age, heavily influenced by his family’s active lifestyle and frequent trips to the mountains. He first started riding in Southern California, where he grew up skateboarding alongside snowboarding, a combination that shaped his unique style. White attended Carlsbad High School but focused early on turning professional, balancing a growing career in skateboarding and snowboarding.

Outside of competition, Shaun is passionate about music and philanthropy. He plays guitar in his band, Bad Things, and is involved in charitable efforts through the Shaun White Foundation, supporting initiatives like St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital. His diverse interests have also led him to pursue business ventures, including his clothing line and collaboration with various brands.

Olympic Highlights

Shaun White made history with five Olympic appearances, debuting at the Turin 2006 Olympic Games where he won his first gold medal in the men’s halfpipe. He continued his dominance with gold medals at the Vancouver 2010 Olympic Games and PyeongChang 2018 Olympic Games. In the Beijing 2022 Olympic Games, he concluded his competitive career with a fourth-place finish in the halfpipe, narrowly missing the podium but earning global admiration for his legacy.

U.S. Championship Highlights

Shaun White's notable U.S. championship highlights include his success at the Burton U.S. Open Snowboarding Championships, where he won multiple titles in the halfpipe. He claimed three consecutive halfpipe championships, with notable victories such as his 2008 win at Stratton Mountain Resort, Vermont.

Career Highlights

Throughout his career, Shaun has been recognized for his technical precision and groundbreaking tricks, including perfect scores at prestigious events like the X Games, where he won 15 gold medals. His ability to push boundaries led him to redefine the standards of competitive snowboarding.

Early Career

Shaun White’s early career was marked by rapid success. By the age of 7, he was already sponsored, and by 13, he had turned pro. His early training at Southern California’s San Bernardino Mountains provided the foundation for his innovative style. He also briefly pursued professional skateboarding, competing in the X Games before focusing entirely on snowboarding.

Olympic Experience
  • 5-time Olympian; 3-time Olympic medalist (3 gold)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 4th (Halfpipe - Men)
    • Olympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, gold (Halfpipe - Men)
    • Olympic Games Sochi 2014, 4th (Halfpipe - Men)
    • Olympic Games Vancouver 2010, gold (Halfpipe - Men)
    • Olympic Games Turin 2006, gold (Halfpipe - Men)

  • Years of participation: Park - Men 2019
  • Top finish: 13th – 2019 (Park - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Grew up in Macon, Georgia after being adopted from China at 18 months
  • Goes by MeiMei, pronounced 'May-May', which was the name given to her by her nannies in China meaning 'beautiful'
  • Born with proximal femoral focal deficiency of her right leg and has had several surgeries on that leg
  • An above the knee amputee, she began swimming at age 5 with coach Fred Lamback
  • After spending five years swimming and training in Orlando, she has been swimming with the Athens Bulldog Swim Club since 2020
  • Has competed in the UK, Berlin, Singapore, Canada, and Peru
  • Daughter of Michael and Kelley White
  • Has one brother, Keegan, and one sister, Mackenzie
  • Hobbies include traveling, reading, cooking, photography, listening to music, spending time with family, and drawing
  • Has two black labs, Radar and Yana.

  • 2019 Para Pan American Games, Lima, Peru, 1st (100 breaststroke), 2nd (400 freestyle), 4th (200 individual medley)

bio.education: Valley High School (Sacramento, Calif.) '94 California State University, Northridge '98, Criminology
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Whitmore has always been an athlete. When she was 5 years old, she started swimming, then it was softball and volleyball before she found her niche in track and field in eighth grade.
  • She quickly found success as a distance runner and earned a scholarship to California State University, Northridge where she ran cross country and track.
  • After graduating in 1998, she decided she wanted to be a professional triathlete. By 2001, she was racing mountain bikes professionally, and one year later she won her first Xterra off-road triathlon.
  • Whitmore went on to become the most successful female athlete in XTERRA history with 37 wins, six national titles and one world title.
  • In March of 2008, Whitmore learned that the source of the lingering pain in her leg was cancer – a spindle cell sarcoma that had wrapped around her sciatic nerve.
  • The next year, Whitmore was in and out of the hospital with surgeries, radiation, chemotherapy and infections that left her fighting for her life. After relearning to walk and giving birth to twin boys in 2010, Whitmore won a gold and silver medal at the Paralympic Games Rio 2016.
  • Daughter of Clyde and Christine Whitmore
  • Has twin sons, Christian and Ryder, who were born in January of 2010
  • Was pregnant with the twins before being a year cancer free
  • She coaches over 30 athletes for off-road and road triathlons
  • Hobbies include mountain biking and riding dirt jumps with her boys and watching Blacklist, Gotham and Hart of Dixie.

Career Highlights
  • Parapan American Games Santiago 2023 - Road - silver (time trial), silver (road race) - track - silver (3000m individual pursuit)

    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, 7th (C1-3 - Road Race - Women, C1-3 - Time Trial - Women), 8th (C1-3 - 3km Pursuit - Women)

bio.education: Casey Wickline was born on March 23, 1986 in Peterborough, New Hampshire. The native of Greenville, South Carolina is a firefighter and a former high school soccer and basketball player. He attended Greenville Technical College. A fellow firefighter told him his brother, who played college football, was trying out in Greenville for the Olympic bobsled team and suggested he also try out. bio.biography:
HEIGHT   6'1"    WEIGHT   240 lbs.


Casey Wickline was born on March 23, 1986 in Peterborough, New Hampshire. The native of Greenville, South Carolina is a firefighter and a former high school soccer and basketball player. He attended Greenville Technical College. A fellow firefighter told him his brother, who played college football, was trying out in Greenville for the Olympic bobsled team and suggested he also try out.


FUN FACTS


- He is a career firefighter/rescue technician. 
- He was able to dunk a basketball by the 7th grade. 
- He loves to spend time with his wife and family.  
- He enjoys hunting, playing golf and woodworking.  
- He has a small lawn care company he runs in the summer's when not at the fire department.  
- He loves Jesus, his wife (Alexis) and two dogs (Chloe, Gabbie.)

  • 2015 World Championships in four-person for pilot Codie Bascue - 20th (3/15)

  • 2014-2015 World Cup in two-man for pilot Codie Bascue - 13th in St. Moritz (1/15)
  • 2014-2015 World Cup in two-man for pilot Nick Cunningham - BRONZE in Lake Placid (12/14); 13th in Calgary (12/14); 11th in La Plagne (1/15); 13th in Igls (2/15)
  • 2014-2015 World Cup in four-person for pilot Nick Cunningham - 5th in Lake Placid (12/14); 12th in Calgary (12/14); 17th in Altenberg (1/15); 15th in Igls (2/15)

bio.education: Bridgeport High School (Bridgeport, Nebraska) ‘96 Chadron State College (Chadron, Nebraska), Elementary Education, University of Nebraska (Lincoln, Nebraska), Elementary Education
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Widhalm, a former collegiate-level wrestler, is no stranger to the elite sports world. As a U.S. Army sniper on his second deployment to Iraq in 2006, he suffered severe injuries due to multiple concussions from IED blasts, and a drop from a helicopter. With the introduction of cycling into his rehabilitation, Widhalm’s condition began to improve. He started on a handcycle in 2009, which he was told would be as far as he would go. Fueled by passion, the lifelong athlete set out to prove the experts wrong. Widhalm worked tirelessly to improve his neuro-muscular and balance problems. He competed in his first Track Cycling National Championships in 2010 placing third. Since then, he has competed at multiple world championships and continues to improve on the national and international level.
  • Son of Dale and Brenda Widhalm
  • Has two brothers, Jacob and Conner
  • Has two children, Conner and Londyn
  • Married to Jennifer Lynn Widhalm
  • Hobbies include spending time with his family, cross-country skiing, inspiring others, discovering his true potential, mentoring others and ice bathing
  • Enjoys shooting as a way of relaxation and to help calm his nerves
  • Was a national wrestling champion in high school
  • Named after the lead singer Justin Hayward of Moody Blues.
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – 4th (C4 - 1000m Time Trial - Men), 6th (C1-5 Team Sprint - Open)
  • Years of participation: C1-5 Team Sprint - Open 2014, 2018, 2022; C4 - 1000m Time Trial - Men 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2022; C4 - 4km Pursuit - Men 2014, 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020; C4 - Omnium - Men 2020; C4 - Scratch Race - Men 2016, 2019, 2020; C4-5 - Scratch Race - Men 2015
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)
    • Bronze – 2019 (C4 - 1000m Time Trial - Men)
Career Highlights
  • ParaPan American Games, Lima, 2019 (C1-5 - 1000m Time Trial - Men, C4-5 - 4km Pursuit - Men, C4-5 - Road Race - Men, C1-5 - Time Trial - Men)
  • ParaPan American Games, Toronto, 2015 (C4-5 - Road Race - Men)
